text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old female with metastatic melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant, Stage IV M1c. Currently on dabrafenib and trametinib (initiated approximately 8 weeks prior). Presenting for first restaging assessment. Known disease sites at baseline: bilateral axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy, multiple cutaneous and subcutaneous nodules over trunk and extremities, and multiple hepatic metastases. Prior staging PET-CT available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast with portal venous phase imaging through the abdomen/pelvis and standard CT timing through the chest. Axial reconstructions at 5 mm with coronal and sagittal reformats. Dose modulation employed.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No focal consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pulmonary nodules identified. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. The central airways are patent. No endobronchial lesions.

Mediastinum: The heart is normal in size. There is no pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta and great vessels are unremarkable. No anterior or middle mediastinal adenopathy. Prevascular, paratracheal, and subcarinal nodal stations are within normal size limits.

Axilla: Right axillary lymph nodes — markedly decreased in size compared with prior. The dominant previously hypermetabolic right axillary node that measured 4.2 cm at baseline has decreased markedly to approximately 1.1 cm along its short axis. The nodal morphology is more ovoid with preserved fatty hilum. Left axillary adenopathy has similarly regressed; a previously enlarged left axillary node measuring 1.8 cm is now 0.6 cm and borderline in size. No new axillary adenopathy.

Soft Tissues / Chest Wall: The cutaneous and subcutaneous nodules over the trunk and bilateral upper extremities that were prominent on the prior staging study have shown dramatic reduction. The previously largest subcutaneous nodule (measured at 3.1 cm) has diminished to approximately 0.7 cm. Multiple additional soft tissue nodules that were identified at baseline are no longer identified on the current study. No new soft tissue masses or skin thickening.

Bones (chest): No lytic or blastic osseous lesions. No pathologic compression fractures.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver: Markedly improved compared with prior. At baseline, six discrete hepatic lesions were identified throughout both lobes. On the current examination, three of these lesions are no longer identified. The previously largest lesion in segment 6 of the right hepatic lobe, which measured 2.4 cm at baseline, has decreased substantially to approximately 0.9 cm — it is now a small hypodense focus with relatively indistinct margins, no rim enhancement, and no satellite nodularity. Two additional previously identified right lobe lesions have similarly decreased by an estimated 55-70%; residual hypodense foci are present but markedly attenuated in size compared with baseline. The left lobe was clear of lesions at baseline and remains so. The liver parenchyma otherwise appears homogeneous. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein is patent.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: Unremarkable. No cholelithiasis. No biliary duct dilation.

Pancreas: Normal in appearance throughout. No pancreatic ductal dilation or peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands: No adrenal masses or nodules identified bilaterally.

Kidneys and Ureters: Normal bilateral renal parenchymal enhancement. No renal masses, hydronephrosis, or calculi. No ureteral dilation.

Bladder: Normal wall thickness and contour. No intraluminal filling defects.

Peritoneum and Retroperitoneum: No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or omental cake. Retroperitoneal and mesenteric nodal stations are within normal size limits with no pathologically enlarged nodes.

Lymph Nodes — Inguinal / Pelvic: Previously identified bilateral inguinal adenopathy has substantially regressed. The right inguinal node that measured 2.1 cm at baseline is now 0.6 cm with a predominantly fatty appearance. The left inguinal node previously measuring 1.4 cm is no longer identified. Pelvic sidewall and iliac nodal chains are unremarkable.

Bowel: No small or large bowel obstruction. Wall thickness is normal throughout. No acute mesenteric or colonic inflammatory changes.

Musculoskeletal / Osseous (abdomen/pelvis): No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions. No soft tissue masses associated with the osseous structures.

Vascular: Aorta and major abdominal branches are normal in caliber. No aneurysmal dilatation.

---

LABORATORY CORRELATION (provided by clinical team): LDH 187 U/L (previously 312 U/L at baseline — now within normal limits).

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Dramatic interval reduction in disease burden across all previously identified sites of involvement compared with the staging PET-CT performed approximately 8 weeks prior. The findings are consistent with a robust and rapid treatment response to dabrafenib/trametinib.

2. Hepatic disease: Substantial reduction in size of the dominant segment 6 hepatic lesion (from 2.4 cm to approximately 0.9 cm). Three of the six previously identified hepatic lesions are no longer visualized. The remaining residual hepatic foci are markedly attenuated and may represent early regressing disease or evolving necrotic change.

3. Nodal disease: Right axillary lymph nodes decreased from 4.2 cm to 1.1 cm; left axillary from 1.8 cm to 0.6 cm. Bilateral inguinal adenopathy substantially resolved — left inguinal node no longer identified, right has decreased markedly.

4. Cutaneous/subcutaneous disease: Dominant nodule decreased from 3.1 cm to 0.7 cm; multiple previously identified soft tissue nodules no longer visible.

5. No new sites of disease identified. No pulmonary metastases. No osseous lesions. No brain imaging performed on this study (brain was clear by MRI at staging; correlate clinically if CNS symptoms develop).

6. Normalization of LDH to 187 U/L from a baseline of 312 U/L, consistent with treatment response.

Overall, the imaging findings reflect a striking reduction in tumor burden across all compartments, including liver, lymph nodes, and soft tissues, consistent with the known rapid early activity of BRAF/MEK inhibition in BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma.

Electronic signature: [Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging / Oncologic Imaging Division]
